paper_title: SciOrch: Learning to Orchestrate Expert LLMs for Solving Frontier Multimodal Scientific Reasoning Tasks

sections.title: Abstract
sections.content: Frontier scientific reasoning remains a major challenge for large language models (LLMs), where even the strongest commercial systems fall short of expert-level performance. A closer look at model behavior reveals substantial complementarity that single-model evaluation hides: different frontier models excel on different question types, and no single model captures the full picture. We present SciOrch, a framework that trains a lightweight 8B model to orchestrate frontier LLMs for scientific reasoning. The orchestrator decomposes each question, delegates sub-problems to selected commercial models through API calls, and synthesizes a final answer. Training such an orchestrator is fundamentally harder than conventional agentic RL: each action triggers an API call that is expensive in both dollar cost and latency, making standard online rollouts infeasible. We address this with MCTS-based approach, producing diverse orchestration trajectories, extracting per-node single-turn samples, and optimizing the orchestrator with GRPO-style training. On a 240-question test set spanning SGI-Reasoning and Scientists' First Exam, SciOrch reaches 56.66% average accuracy, outperforming the strongest single commercial model by 3.74% and the strongest multi-agent baseline by 3.33%. It also attains the best accuracy on both SGI and SFE with less than half the API cost of typical multi-agent methods.

sections.title: Start
sections.content: 2026-06-09 SciOrch: Learning to Orchestrate Expert LLMs for Solving Frontier Multimodal Scientific Reasoning Tasks Jingru Guo1, Xiangyuan Xue2, Lian Zhang3, Wanghan Xu4,5, Siki Chen6, Philip Torr6, Wanli Ouyang2,5,7, Lei Bai5, Zhenfei Yin6 2The Chinese University of Hong Kong 1Imperial College London 3University of Illinois Urbana-Champaign 5Shanghai Artificial Intelligence Laboratory 4Shanghai Jiao Tong University 7Shenzhen Loop Area Institute 6University of Oxford 6 2 0 J 4 1 ] . [ 1 2 7 8 5 1 . 6 0 6 2 : r # jingru.guo25@imperial.ac.uk Corresponding authors. https://github.com/llexieguo/SciOrch Abstract Frontier scientific reasoning remains major challenge for large language models (LLMs), where even the strongest commercial systems fall short of expert-level performance. closer look at model behavior reveals substantial complementarity that single-model evaluation hides: different frontier models excel on different question types, and no single model captures the full picture. We present SciOrch, framework that trains lightweight 8B model to orchestrate frontier LLMs for scientific reasoning. The orchestrator decomposes each question, delegates sub-problems to selected commercial models through API calls, and synthesizes final answer. Training such an orchestrator is fundamentally harder than conventional agentic RL: each action triggers an API call that is expensive in both dollar cost and latency, making standard online rollouts infeasible. We address this with MCTS-based approach, producing diverse orchestration trajectories, extracting per-node single-turn samples, and optimizing the orchestrator with GRPO-style training. On 240-question test set spanning SGI-Reasoning and Scientists First Exam, SciOrch reaches 56.66% average accuracy, outperforming the strongest single commercial model by 3.74% and the strongest multi-agent baseline by 3.33%. It also attains the best accuracy on both SGI and SFE with less than half the API cost of typical multi-agent methods. 1. Introduction Multimodal scientific reasoning is one of the most demanding tasks for large language models (LLMs) because of the urgent need for accelerating scientific discovery. It requires interpreting experimental figures, integrating knowledge across sub-fields, and reaching conclusions that an experienced researcher would accept. However, even the most powerful LLMs cannot reliably solve those frontier problems. SGI-Reasoning, subset of multimodal reasoning tasks from the recent SGI-Bench [Xu et al., 2025], captures this setting. On the frontier tasks involving nine disciplines ranging from astronomy to material science, even the strongest commercial model, Gemini-3-Pro [Google, 2025], reaches only 42% accuracy on this task. Yet this ceiling masks more nuanced picture. We evaluate broad range of frontier models on SGI-Reasoning and find substantial disagreement among their correct answers (Figure 1): combining just three models (Gemini-3-Pro, Gemini-2.5-Pro [Comanici et al., 2025], and GPT-4o [OpenAI, 2024]) already covers 74% of the test set, almost double the best single-model accuracy, and expanding the pool to all evaluated models pushes the union to 89%. This 89% is an oracle upper bound, since it assumes per-instance knowledge of which model is correct, but it shows how much capability already exists in the current model pool. Different models also lead on different disciplines: Claude-Sonnet-4 [Anthropic, 2025a] ranks first on SciOrch: Learning to Orchestrate Expert LLMs for Solving Frontier Multimodal Scientific Reasoning Tasks Astronomy, o4-mini [OpenAI, 2025] on Earth Science and Physics, and Gemini-3-Pro on Life Science. The complementarity is not only across questions. typical SGI-Reasoning question mixes figure parsing, domain-specific recall, and quantitative reasoning, and these sub-tasks do not always favor the same model: one model may read plot more reliably while another handles the underlying mechanism better. The headroom therefore comes not from building stronger individual model, but from combining existing ones at granularity finer than whole-question dispatch. Existing approaches to exploiting model diversity fall into two families, each with structural limitations. Prompt-based multi-agent methods (Figure 2a) such as self-consistency [Wang et al., 2023b] and multi-agent debate [Du et al., 2024] aggregate multiple reasoning paths, but their protocols are static: cost grows linearly with the number of paths while accuracy gains saturate quickly, and the same procedure is applied regardless of question difficulty, discipline, or model strengths. Routing-based methods (Figure 2b), such as prompt-to-leaderboard [Frick et al., 2025], train router to dispatch each query to single pre-selected model, but the decision is committed once and cannot adapt to intermediate evidence or decompose the query across models. separate line of work introduces orchestration [Ruan et al., 2026, Su et al., 2025, Zhang et al., 2026a], where orchestrator learns to decompose query and route its sub-problems across models. This fits the within-question complementarity, but existing methods target general tool use and have never been extended to frontier scientific reasoning. Figure 1 Complementarity among frontier LLMs on SGI-Reasoning. Top: oracle accuracy when pooling the top-𝑁 frontier models. Bottom: per-domain accuracy and oracle envelopes for the best three and all models. Inspired by orchestration-based methods, we propose SciOrch (Figure 2c), framework that trains lightweight 8B vision-language model (VLM) to orchestrate frontier commercial models. The orchestrator treats commercial model APIs as tools in its action space. At each step it chooses between two actions: delegate sub-problem to specified model, or submit final answer. This formulation turns long-context multimodal reasoning into multi-step decision problem. To achieve learning, reinforcement learning (RL) is the most intuitive way, but it is extremely diffcult in this case for two reasons: (i) every action triggers real API call, which makes large-scale rollouts expensive; and (ii) each call adds non-trivial latency, which makes standard online RL prohibitively slow. To achieve balance between this performance-efficiency trade-off, we integrate Monte Carlo Tree Search (MCTS) [Browne et al., 2012, Wu et al., 2025] in our training. It rollouts diverse orchestration trajectories, from which we extract per-node single-turn samples and update the model with REINFORCE++ [Hu et al., 2025]. In this way, single rollout yields much more training samples within the same cost of API call. The gradient phase runs offline on sufficient pre-collected samples, significantly improving the training efficiency while introducing acceptable data staleness. Empirically, SciOrch turns this complementarity into measurable gains. On the 240-question test set, it achieves the best accuracy on both evaluation slices, with 49.30% on SGI-Reasoning [Xu et al., 2025] and 68.10% on Scientists First Exam [Zhou et al., 2025], yielding 56.66% average accuracy. This improves over the strongest standalone commercial model, Gemini-3-Pro, by 3.74%, and over 2 SciOrch: Learning to Orchestrate Expert LLMs for Solving Frontier Multimodal Scientific Reasoning Tasks Figure 2 Comparison of multi-agent reasoning paradigms. (a) Prompt-based methods such as self-consistency and multi-agent debate apply fixed protocol regardless of question difficulty, with their cost growing linearly and accuracy saturating quickly. (b) Routing-based methods send each query to single pre-selected model based on prompt features, but the router can neither revise its choice after seeing intermediate evidence, nor decompose the query into sub-problems handled by different models. (c) SciOrch trains lightweight orchestrator to decompose each question, delegate sub-problems to the most suitable commercial model, and submit final answer once evidence is sufficient, enabling adaptive cross-model orchestration with selective routing and early stopping. the strongest prompt-based multi-agent baseline by 3.33%. The improvement is not simply purchased with more API calls: SciOrch costs only $10.42 over the full test set, which is much lower than the best self-consistency baselines. To sum up, the core contributions of our work are three-fold: 1. We propose SciOrch, an orchestration framework for frontier scientific reasoning that frames the task as multi-step decision problem, where lightweight agent learns to delegate sub-problems to frontier models. 2. We introcue an MCTS-based method that makes RL tractable in the orchestration setting, where tree search generates sufficient, diverse trajectories, and offline gradient updates decouple training from API latency. 3. Experiments on 240-question test set across nine scientific disciplines demonstrate that SciOrch surpasses the strongest commercial model at substantially lower inference cost. 2. Related Work 2.1. Benchmarks for Scientific Reasoning Early benchmarks for scientific reasoning covered broad academic knowledge through multiplechoice questions and are now largely saturated by frontier models [Clark et al., 2018, Hendrycks et al., 2021, Welbl et al., 2017]. Later benchmarks raised the difficulty bar with graduate-level evaluations that resist shallow retrieval, on which even the strongest reasoning models reach only 50 SciOrch: Learning to Orchestrate Expert LLMs for Solving Frontier Multimodal Scientific Reasoning Tasks to 60 percent accuracy [Feng et al., 2024, Huang et al., 2024, M-A-P Team et al., 2025, Rein et al., 2024]. As vision-language models matured, new generation of multimodal scientific benchmarks emerged to test the interpretation of figures, diagrams, and experimental data [He et al., 2024, Lu et al., 2022, Wang et al., 2024b, Yue et al., 2024, 2025a, Zhou et al., 2025]. Among these, frontier multimodal scientific reasoning, where each question requires interpreting real experimental figures across multiple disciplines, remains the hardest, and is the setting we target through SGI-Bench. 2.2. LLM-based Multi-agent Reasoning growing body of work tries to push past the limits of any single model by combining multiple models or multiple reasoning paths [Guo et al., 2024, Li et al., 2024, Tran et al., 2025]. Promptbased methods aggregate sampled reasoning paths through majority voting, debate, or structured collaboration [Chan et al., 2024, Liang et al., 2024, Qian et al., 2025, Wang et al., 2024a, Wei et al., 2022]; their protocols are fixed, inference cost grows linearly with the number of paths, and accuracy gains saturate quickly. second line of work trains router that dispatches each query to single model from pool, trading off answer quality against inference cost [Chen et al., 2023, 2024, Ding et al., 2024, Feng et al., 2025a,b, Frick et al., 2025, Yue et al., 2025b, Zhang et al., 2026b], but the decision is made upfront from the prompt alone and cannot adapt to intermediate evidence or decompose the query across models. more recent direction trains small orchestrator to delegate stronger models or tools over multiple steps [Hu et al., 2024, Ruan et al., 2026, Shang et al., 2024, Su et al., 2025, Zhang et al., 2024a,b, 2026a, Zhuge et al., 2024], typically via RL under verifiable rewards [DeepSeek-AI et al., 2025, Shao et al., 2024], sometimes combined with tree search. Most of this work targets general tool use or open-ended agentic tasks, while our setting is different, since each action triggers an expensive commercial API call rather than cheap forward pass. 2.3. Agents for Scientific Discovery separate line of research, often grouped under AI for Science, builds domain-specialized systems that act as scientific agents [AI4Science and Quantum, 2023, Miret and Krishnan, 2024, Wang et al., 2023a]. Some systems train predictive models for specific scientific tasks, such as protein structure, materials, and chemistry [Abramson et al., 2024, Baek et al., 2021, Jumper et al., 2021, Merchant et al., 2023, Szymanski et al., 2023]. Others pair LLMs with search or evolutionary procedures to discover algorithms, equations, or mathematical constructions [Novikov et al., 2025, Romera-Paredes et al., 2024, Schmidt and Lipson, 2009], and third group builds full agentic pipelines that walk through hypothesis generation, experimental design, literature review, and wet-lab validation [Boiko et al., 2023, Bran et al., 2024, Gottweis et al., 2025, Lu et al., 2024]. These systems lean on domainspecific modules and target the full scientific process. Our work is complementary: we focus on the general reasoning step that almost any such pipeline ultimately depends on, by training small orchestrator that combines the strengths of frontier LLMs on multimodal scientific reasoning. 3. Method 3.1. Orchestration Architecture We frame multimodal scientific reasoning tasks as multi-step decision problem. Given multimodal question (𝐼, 𝑞) with set of images 𝐼 = {𝑖1, . . . , 𝑖𝑛} of variable size 𝑛 0 and text query 𝑞, an orchestrator 𝜋𝜃 interacts with pool of commercial models accessed through APIs: = {𝑚1, 𝑚2, . . . }. (1) SciOrch: Learning to Orchestrate Expert LLMs for Solving Frontier Multimodal Scientific Reasoning Tasks Figure 3 Three-phase MCTS for orchestrator training. (a) Step-level action. At each step, the orchestrator 𝜋𝜃 takes the current state 𝑠𝑡 = (𝐼, 𝑞𝑡, ℎ𝑡) and emits one JSON-formatted action, either delegate to commercial model or submit of the final answer. Each node in the MCTS tree corresponds to one such action. (b) Phase 1: diverse rollout. Build binary tree in two steps: (1) at each node, two prompts with different model pools each sample 𝑛 candidate actions, and the pair with the largest sentence-embedding cosine distance over the action text is selected as the two children; (2) rollout from each leaf continues until all paths terminate. (c) Phase 2: signaldriven expansion. Grow the tree to 𝑁 leaves by repeatedly expanding the node with the highest score(𝑣) = Δ𝑄(𝑣) 𝑐 depth(𝑣). (d) Phase 3: pruning. Keep at most 𝐾 children per parent, chosen to maximize the parents expected return. The orchestrator is lightweight vision-language model that breaks each question into sub-problems, sends each sub-problem to chosen model in M, and writes final answer from the responses it collects. Figure 2(c) shows the full process. At step 𝑡, the state contains the original question together with what has happened so far: 𝑠𝑡 = (𝐼, 𝑞, ℎ𝑡), ℎ𝑡 = {(𝑚𝑖, 𝑞𝑖, 𝑟𝑖)}𝑖<𝑡, (2) where ℎ𝑡 stores every previous delegation: which model was called, what sub-question was sent, and what response came back. Conditioned on 𝑠𝑡, the orchestrator emits single action in JSON-like format. The action space is: = Adelegate {𝑎submit}. (3) delegate action specifies the target model 𝑚 and an instruction 𝑞 that phrases the sub-question, along with sub-task tag drawn from {calculation, sci-reasoning, vision-reasoning}. The selected model returns textual response 𝑟 via its API, and the resulting tuple (𝑚, 𝑞, 𝑟) is appended to ℎ𝑡. submit action carries the final answer 𝑎 and ends the episode. Outputs are constrained by structured prompting, and malformed actions are rejected during sampling. Figure 3a illustrates this step-level action structure with concrete JSON examples for both action types. The pool contains diverse frontier models with complementary strengths. The orchestrator commits to single model per delegation instead of querying every model in parallel, so it has to learn from 5 SciOrch: Learning to Orchestrate Expert LLMs for Solving Frontier Multimodal Scientific Reasoning Tasks experience which model is good at which kind of sub-question. This is what makes the system selective routing rather than exhaustive ensembling. 3.2. Monte Carlo Tree Search Training such an orchestrator with standard online RL is impractical since each rollout requires API calls. If we adopt classical online RL, the training cost and latency will explode. Therefore, we adopt MCTS to generate diverse trajectories within each iteration, so that single tree yields many state-action samples by sharing the cost of common prefixes across branches. As shown in Figure 3, our MCTS runs in three phases: diverse strategy rollout, signal-driven expansion, and pruning. Tree structure. Multiple trajectories sampled from task form tree G. Each node 𝑣 in the tree corresponds to state 𝑠𝑣. Edges represent actions sampled from the current policy 𝜋𝜃. The root is the initial state 𝑠0 = (𝐼, 𝑞, ), and each leaf is either submit node or node that reaches the step budget. Phase 1: diverse rollout. Starting from the root, we construct each nodes two children in two steps (Figure 3b). (1) Candidate generation. We issue two prompts to the orchestrator, each exposing different subset of the model pool, so that the candidate actions cover distinct routing options at the source. Each prompt samples 𝑛 candidate actions, yielding 𝑚 candidates in total. (2) Farthest-pair selection. From these 𝑚 candidates, we select the pair with the largest sentence-embedding cosine distance over the action text, and use them as the two children of 𝑣. The two steps target diversity at different stages, generation and selection, and together produce an initial tree whose siblings tend to disagree on routing choices. We continue rolling out from each leaf until all paths terminate. Phase 2: signal-driven expansion. Previous works have shown that GRPO-style [Shao et al., 2024] methods benefit from states where different actions yield clearly different returns: the larger the return gap across siblings, the stronger the relative-advantage signal driving policy updates. We therefore introduce signal-driven strategy that enriches such informative states until the tree contains 𝑁 leaves (Figure 3c). At each expansion step, we select the node 𝑣 that maximizes: where 𝑐 is depth-penalty coefficient and Δ𝑄(𝑣) is the return gap among the children of 𝑣: score(𝑣) = Δ𝑄(𝑣) 𝑐 depth(𝑣), Δ𝑄(𝑣) = max 𝑣 children(𝑣) 𝑄(𝑣) min 𝑣 children(𝑣) 𝑄(𝑣), (4) (5) and 𝑄(𝑣) = 𝔼𝜏𝑣 [𝑅(𝜏)] is the empirical expected return at 𝑣. Parents with large Δ𝑄 carry the strongest training signal, while the depth penalty discourages overly deep, narrow branches and keeps the tree balanced. The selected node is then expanded with the same procedure as in Phase 1. Phase 3: Pruning. Phase 2 may attach many children to single parent, and some of these children can be redundant: they either share the same routing decision in different wording or fall into degenerate all-correct or all-wrong sibling groups, neither of which produces useful gradients (Figure 3d). For any node with more than 𝐾 children, we keep subset of size 𝐾 chosen to maximize the parents expected return: C(𝑣) = arg max children(𝑣), =𝐾 1 𝑄(𝑣). 𝑣 (6) 3.3. Step-Level Training straightforward use of MCTS data is to treat each root-to-leaf trajectory as one sample and apply trajectory-level policy gradients. We find this unstable in practice: trajectories sharing common SciOrch: Learning to Orchestrate Expert LLMs for Solving Frontier Multimodal Scientific Reasoning Tasks prefix receive different returns based on later decisions, so the same early action appears in samples with conflicting rewards, leading to noisy gradients. We instead train on individual nodes under non-cumulative setting. For each non-leaf node 𝑣 in the tree G, we form single-turn training sample (𝑠𝑣, 𝑎𝑣, 𝑅𝑣), where 𝑎𝑣 is the action taken from 𝑣 and 𝑅𝑣 is the empirical return signal extracted from MCTS. We define 𝑅𝑣 as the value of the child reached by 𝑎𝑣, baselined against the average value of all explored siblings: 𝑅𝑣 = 𝑄(𝑣child) 𝑉 (𝑣), (7) 1 where 𝑄(𝑣child) = 𝔼𝜏𝑣child [𝑅(𝜏)] is the empirical expected return at the child, and 𝑉 (𝑣) = children(𝑣) (cid:205)𝑣 children(𝑣) 𝑄(𝑣) serves as baseline computed over siblings explored under MCTS. This decomposes the trajectory-level signal into per-step credit, with each sample reflecting how much the chosen action improves over the average alternative. Samples collected across all training questions in iteration 𝑡 form an on-policy training batch T𝑡 that drives the policy update. Policy optimization. Many recent RL methods for LLMs adopt GRPO and its variants, which rely on multiple rollouts from single prompt to estimate group-relative advantages. In our setting, the relevant group is the set of sibling actions explored at the same MCTS node, and 𝑅𝑣 already encodes the within-group relative return. We therefore adopt REINFORCE++, which is naturally compatible with this per-node formulation. For each sample (𝑠𝑣, 𝑎𝑣, 𝑅𝑣) T𝑡, we compute token-level advantage by combining the node-level return with per-token KL penalty against fixed reference policy 𝜋ref: 𝐴(ℓ) 𝑣 = 𝑅𝑣 𝛽 𝑎𝑣 𝑗=ℓ log 𝑣 𝜋𝜃𝑡 (𝑎( 𝑗) 𝜋ref(𝑎( 𝑗) 𝑣 𝑠𝑣, 𝑎(< 𝑗) ) 𝑣 𝑠𝑣, 𝑎(< 𝑗) 𝑣 ) , (8) where 𝑎(ℓ) 𝑣 denotes the ℓ-th token of the action, 𝑎𝑣 is the action length in tokens, and 𝛽 controls the strength of the KL constraint. The reference policy 𝜋ref is fixed to the initial checkpoint and prevents the orchestrator from drifting too far from sensible language model. To stabilize updates across nodes with different return scales, we normalize advantages across the batch: ˆ𝐴(ℓ) 𝑣 = 𝑣 Mean({ 𝐴(ℓ) 𝐴(ℓ) Std({ 𝐴(ℓ) 𝑣 }) + 𝜖 𝑣 }) . Surrogate objective. For each sample, let 𝜌(ℓ) 𝑣 (𝜃𝑡) = 𝑣 𝜋𝜃𝑡 (𝑎(ℓ) 𝜋𝜃old (𝑎(ℓ) 𝑣 𝑠𝑣, 𝑎(<ℓ) ) 𝑣 𝑠𝑣, 𝑎(<ℓ) 𝑣 ) (9) (10) denote the per-token importance-sampling ratio, where 𝜋𝜃old optimize 𝜋𝜃𝑡 with clipped surrogate objective in the style of PPO, applied at the token level: is the policy used to collect T𝑡. We (𝜃𝑡) = 𝔼T𝑡 (cid:34) 𝑎𝑣 ℓ= min (cid:16) 𝜌(ℓ) 𝑣 ˆ𝐴(ℓ) 𝑣 , clip(𝜌(ℓ) 𝑣 , 1 𝜖, 1 + 𝜖) ˆ𝐴(ℓ) 𝑣 (cid:35) (cid:17) . (11) The clipping constrains each update to remain within trusted region around 𝜋𝜃old for stability when training on samples that mix correct and incorrect API responses. , which is critical Iterative training. Each iteration 𝑡 consists of three phases: (1) collect an on-policy batch T𝑡 by over set of training questions; (2) compute running three-phase MCTS under the current policy 𝜋𝜃𝑡 7 SciOrch: Learning to Orchestrate Expert LLMs for Solving Frontier Multimodal Scientific Reasoning Tasks Algorithm 1 Training Process of SciOrch. Require: Initial policy 𝜋𝜃0, training set D, model pool M, iterations 𝑇 Ensure: Trained orchestrator 𝜋𝜃𝑇 1: for 𝑡 = 0, 1, . . . , 𝑇 1 do // Sampling phase Sample batch {(𝐼𝑖, 𝑞𝑖, 𝑎 for each (𝐼𝑖, 𝑞𝑖, 𝑎 𝑖=1 D; 2: 3: 𝑖 )}𝐵 𝑖 ) in the batch do ; 𝜋𝜃𝑡 , M) G𝑖 MCTS(𝐼𝑖, 𝑞𝑖, 𝑎 𝑖 for each non-leaf node 𝑣 G𝑖 do ˆ𝐴𝑣 𝑄(𝑣child) 𝑉 (𝑣); {(𝑠𝑣, 𝑎𝑣, ˆ𝐴𝑣)} 4: 5: 6: 7: 8: 9: end for end for // Update phase 𝐴𝑣 ( ˆ𝐴𝑣 𝜇 T)/𝜎T for all 𝑣 (𝜃𝑡) 1 (cid:205)𝑣 10: 11: end for 𝐴𝑣 log 𝜋𝜃𝑡 (𝑎𝑣 𝑠𝑣); 𝜃𝑡+1 𝜃𝑡 𝜂𝜃𝑡 (𝜃𝑡) per-token advantages 𝐴(ℓ) 𝑣 with the KL penalty and normalize them across T𝑡; (3) update 𝜃 on the clipped surrogate (𝜃𝑡). The updated checkpoint becomes the policy for the next iterations MCTS sampling, keeping the procedure on-policy while amortizing API cost across many per-node samples. Algorithm 1 summarizes the full procedure. 4. Experiments 4.1. Dataset Construction We construct training and test split that combines SGI-Reasoning [Xu et al., 2025], our primary evaluation benchmark, with Scientists First Exam [Zhou et al., 2025] as difficultyand modalitymatched augmentation source. SGI subset (145 train / 146 test). SGI-Reasoning contains 291 questions. We stratify by discipline and split into 145 training and 146 held-out test questions. The training subset preserves the discipline distribution of the full benchmark. SFE subset (31 train / 94 test). SFE contains 830 bilingual visual question-answering items. We choose SFE as the augmentation source for four reasons: (1) it is natively English with real scientific image; (2) its difficulty matches SGI-Reasoning with the o3 model [OpenAI, 2025] reaching about 34% accuracy on SFE, comparable to the 42% reached by Gemini-3-Pro [Google, 2025] on SGIReasoning; (3) it shares the multimodal modality and scientific-figure framing of SGI; and (4) it does not contain training-contamination canaries. We apply three-step filter to extract usable samples. First, we keep only English multiple-choice items, yielding 281 candidates. Second, we apply quality gating step. Each candidate is evaluated by five frontier models, including GPT-5.4 [OpenAI, 2026], GPT-4o [OpenAI, 2024], Gemini-3-Pro, Gemini-2.5-Pro [Comanici et al., 2025], and Claude-Sonnet-4.5 [Anthropic, 2025b]). sample is retained if at least one model answers correctly. This filter removes items that no frontier model can solve, which are overwhelmingly mislabeled or ambiguous rather than genuinely hard. The resulting pool matches SGI in difficulty without truncating its upper tail. Third, we sample stratified by discipline to fill the test quota first and assign the remaining items to the training set. 8 SciOrch: Learning to Orchestrate Expert LLMs for Solving Frontier Multimodal Scientific Reasoning Tasks Resulting split. Table 1 reports the discipline composition of the final 176-question training set (145 SGI + 31 SFE) and 240-question test set (146 SGI + 94 SFE). The training set preserves the discipline weights of SGI-Reasoning, with SFE augmentation concentrated in the five disciplines it covers. The test set spans all nine SGI disciplines, and the four disciplines not covered by SFE are evaluated solely on SGI, while the other draw from both sources. Discipline Astronomy Chemistry Earth Sci. Energy Information Life Sci. Material Neuroscience Physics SGI 8/9 4/7 28/26 7/3 8/12 43/42 17/21 13/11 17/15 SFE 9/18 9/18 9/18 4/28 0/12 Total 17/27 13/25 37/44 7/3 8/12 47/70 17/33 13/11 17/15 Total 145/146 31/94 176/240 Table 1 Dataset composition by discipline and source (train/test). SFE covers five disciplines with real scientific images; indicates the discipline is not covered by SFE, in which case both training and testing use SGI. Baselines. We compare SciOrch against four categories of baselines on the 240-question test set. (1) Frontier models. We involve GPT-5.4, Gemini-3-Pro, and Claude-Sonnet-4.5, each prompted on the full multimodal input (𝐼, 𝑞). (2) Multi-agent methods. We involve self-consistency [Wang et al., 2023b], which majority-votes over multiple CoT samples, and multi-agent debate [Du et al., 2024], where models exchange and revise answers over fixed rounds. (3) Routing-based methods. We involve prompt-to-leaderboard (P2L) [Frick et al., 2025], which predicts per-query Bradley-Terry win rates and selects the top model. We use the public P2L-1.5B checkpoint with its output head restricted to our model pool. (4) Training-based methods. We conduct SFT [Ouyang et al., 2022] and GRPO [Shao et al., 2024] on the Qwen3-VL-8B-Instruct model [Bai et al., 2025] under the same training set, isolating the contribution of SciOrchs orchestration with RL-based training. 4.2. Implementation Details Orchestrator and model pool. We consistently use Qwen3-VL-8B-Instruct [Bai et al., 2025] as the orchestrator 𝜋𝜃, with model pool of 16 commercial endpoints spanning three families: OpenAI (GPT-5.4, GPT-5.2, GPT-5, GPT-5-mini, GPT-4.1, GPT-4.1-mini, GPT-4o, GPT-4o-mini, o3), Anthropic (Claude-Sonnet-4.5, Claude-Sonnet-4, Claude-Haiku-4.5), and Google (Gemini-3-Pro, Gemini-3-Flash, Gemini-2.5-Pro, Gemini-2.5-Flash). The pool deliberately mixes flagship and lighter variants so that the orchestrator can also learn to trade off accuracy against cost. MCTS sampling. Each Phase 1 prompt samples 𝑛 = 4 candidate actions, so 𝑚 = 2𝑛 = 8 per node. Phase 2 expands until the tree contains 𝑁 = 64 leaves. Phase 3 caps each parent at 𝐾 = 8 children. The depth-penalty coefficient is 𝑐 = 0.5. Training. We implement SciOrch with the MS-Swift framework [Zhao et al., 2025], using its REINFORCE++ [Hu et al., 2025] implementation as the policy optimizer. We train for 𝑇 iterations on the 176-question training set; each iteration generates an on-policy batch via three-phase MCTS and updates the orchestrator on the clipped surrogate objective. The orchestrator is fine-tuned with SciOrch: Learning to Orchestrate Expert LLMs for Solving Frontier Multimodal Scientific Reasoning Tasks SGI SFE Average Cost Method GPT-5.4 Vanilla Consistency Debate Claude-Sonnet-4.5 Vanilla Consistency Debate Gemini-3-Pro Vanilla Consistency Debate Qwen3-VL-8B Vanilla Consistency Debate SFT GRPO 40.41 45.21 39.73 31.51 39.04 40.41 46.58 45.89 45.89 24.70 22.60 21.23 26.00 19.60 67.02 60.64 62.77 48.94 57.45 52. 62.77 64.89 64.89 31.90 43.62 44.68 39.40 41.30 P2L 32.17 57.50 SciOrch (Ours) 49.30 68.10 50.83 51.25 48.75 38.33 46.25 45.00 52.92 53.33 53.33 27.52 30.83 30.42 31.24 29. 37.70 56.66 4.18 24.73 13.22 3.14 19.33 21.72 5.97 14.36 17.65 - - - - - 5.39 10.42 Table 2 Main results on the 240-question test set (accuracy%). Three frontier models and Qwen3VL-8B are evaluated under vanilla prompting, self-consistency, and multi-agent debate. We also report SFT and GRPO on Qwen3-VL-8B as training-based methods and P2L as routing-based method. SciOrch uses Qwen3-VL-8B as an orchestrator with access to pool of frontier models. Average is the weighted mean over 146 SGI and 94 SFE questions. Cost is the total API spend in USD. AdamW at low learning rate of 1e-6, with small KL loss and PPO-style clip. All the training runs on 8 NVIDIA H100 GPUs, but the wall-clock time is dominated by API latency. Evaluation. We report exact-match accuracy on three test slices, i.e., SGI, SFE, and the combined 240-question test set, along with API cost. 4.3. Main Results Table 2 summarizes our findings. The open-source Qwen3-VL-8B substantially trails every frontier model in its vanilla form with poor average accuracy of 27.52%. Continued training does not close this gap: SFT and GRPO yield only performance gains of 3.72% and 1.58% over the vanilla form, still well below every frontier baseline. The picture is consistent across slices: on SGI the post-trained Qwen3-VL-8B reaches at most 26.00% accuracy, and on SFE it tops out at 44.68% accuracy. stronger standalone open-source model is therefore not viable path on this benchmark. Test-time scaling on the frontier models themselves also fails to close the gap. Self-consistency improves GPT-5.4 by only 0.42% and multi-agent debate even degrades it by 2.08%, while inflating API spend by 5 times. Claude-Sonnet-4.5 plateaus at 45.00% under both methods. Across all three frontier models, no one dominates both slices: GPT-5.4 leads on SFE with an accuracy of 67.02% but trails Gemini-3-Pro on SGI. Prompt-based methods on any one model cannot recover the complementary 10 SciOrch: Learning to Orchestrate Expert LLMs for Solving Frontier Multimodal Scientific Reasoning Tasks capabilities held by the others. The routing baseline further confirms this from the other direction: off-the-shelf preference-trained routers stay well below any individual frontier model. SciOrch addresses both limitations. An 8B orchestrator learns to dynamically dispatch among pool of frontier models and attains the highest score on both slices simultaneously, achieving 49.30% accuracy on SGI and 68.10% accuracy on SFE. The average accuracy of 56.66% is 5.41% above the strongest baseline and 5.83% above the strongest vanilla model. The gain is also disproportionate to the cost: SciOrch spends $10.42 across the 240-question test set, which is less than half of the cost of self-consistency based on GPT-5.4, while consistently delivering much better average accuracy. 5. Conclusion In this work, we present SciOrch, framework that trains lightweight model to orchestrate expert models for challenging multimodal scientific reasoning. By formulating the task as multi-step decision problem where each action is an API call, we cast frontier scientific reasoning as routing problem and exploit the cross-model complementarity that single-model evaluation hides. To make the RL-based learning process tractable under the per-action API costs, we propose step-level MCTS-based training, which generates diverse trajectories through tree search and extracts per-node training signals for applying REINFORCE++. Experiments on 240-question test set show that SciOrch surpasses the strongest single commercial model at lower inference cost. We hope this work motivates further study of learnable orchestration as complementary direction for frontier scientific reasoning. 6. Limitations Evaluation scale. Our test set contains 240 questions across nine scientific disciplines. While this is comparable to other frontier scientific reasoning benchmarks at the same difficulty level, the absolute size is modest, and individual per-discipline cells are small. As result, we cannot conclusively verify how well SciOrch generalizes beyond the evaluated distribution, and per-discipline trends should be read as suggestive rather than definitive. Larger-scale evaluation, especially on out-of-distribution scientific tasks, remains future work. Multiple-choice format. Our training and evaluation are restricted to multiple-choice questions, where exact-match correctness against the ground-truth answer provides clean verifiable reward. This is meaningful gap from the open-ended nature of real scientific practice, where an AI scientist would need to generate hypotheses, write free-form derivations, and produce arguments that can only be judged for partial correctness or semantic equivalence. Extending SciOrch to free-form scientific reasoning may require reliable reward model. 7. Ethical Considerations Potential misuse. SciOrch lowers the cost of producing competitive scientific answers by routing lightweight 8B orchestrator across frontier commercial APIs. While this makes scientific reasoning capability more accessible to under-resourced research groups, the same property could be exploited by bad actors. The risk is amplified by the fact that the orchestrator runs only the routing logic, not the underlying frontier weights, which should be released for the system to be reproduced. We urge practitioners deploying orchestration-style systems to combine them with output-side safeguards rather than relying on the cost barrier alone. Model hallucination. SciOrchs answer is synthesized from responses returned by commercial models, each of which is known to hallucinate, especially on frontier scientific questions where ground truth 11 SciOrch: Learning to Orchestrate Expert LLMs for Solving Frontier Multimodal Scientific Reasoning Tasks is scarce in pretraining data. Our orchestrator does not verify the factual content of any individual API response. It routes and aggregates rather than fact-checks. As result, the system can confidently produce answers that are well-formatted, internally coherent, and yet factually wrong, in ways that may be harder to detect than the obvious errors single weaker model would make. SciOrchs answer should be treated as candidate reasoning to be verified, not as substitutes for primary literature or experimental evidence.

In International Conference on Machine Learning (ICML), 2024. 16 SciOrch: Learning to Orchestrate Expert LLMs for Solving Frontier Multimodal Scientific Reasoning Tasks A. The Use of LLMs We used LLMs exclusively for manuscript refinement. Specifically, we used them to correct typographical errors, fix grammatical problems, and improve linguistic expression. The authors carefully reviewed and validated all content generated or refined with LLM assistance. We did not use LLMs for conceptual development, data collection, code implementation, experimental design, or interpretation of results. B. Model Documentation Orchestrator backbone. SciOrch uses Qwen3-VL-8B-Instruct as its policy backbone. At each decision step, the policy observes the original multimodal question, the accumulated interaction history, and the available commercial model pool, then emits structured action: either delegate sub-problem to one commercial model or submit final answer. During training, MCTS samples candidate orchestration trajectories using the current Qwen policy, and REINFORCE++ updates the policy from step-level signals extracted from the resulting search tree. We also evaluate Qwen3-VL-8B-Instruct as an open-source baseline under vanilla prompting, SFT, and GRPO. Expert models. SciOrch uses GPT-5.4, Gemini-3-Pro, and Claude-Sonnet-4.5 as its three commercial expert models. During training and inference, the Qwen policy delegates selected sub-problems to these models through API calls, and each response is appended to the interaction history before the next decision step. We also evaluate the same three models as standalone prompted baselines and run self-consistency and multi-agent debate on this pool. GPT-4o and Gemini-2.5-Pro are used for model-complementarity analysis and SFE quality gating, but they are not reported as prompted standalone baselines in the main result table. Routing baseline. We use the public P2L-1.5B checkpoint as the routing baseline. P2L receives the question stem together with OCR-extracted figure text, then selects one model from the same three-model commercial pool used by SciOrch. This baseline tests whether one-shot textbased routing can match SciOrchs multi-step delegation. C. Dataset Documentation SGI-Reasoning. SGI-Reasoning is the primary benchmark used in this paper. It is subset of SGI-Bench and contains 291 multimodal scientific reasoning questions across nine disciplines. Each example requires reasoning over scientific visual content and selecting an answer in multiple-choice format. We stratify the dataset by discipline into 145 training questions and 146 held-out test questions. We do not apply additional quality gating because it is the target evaluation benchmark. Scientists First Exam. SFE is used as an augmentation source. The full dataset contains 830 bilingual visual question-answering items across Astronomy, Chemistry, Earth Science, Life Science, and Materials Science. We first keep English multiple-choice items, yielding 281 candidate examples. We then apply multi-model quality gating with GPT-5.4, GPT-4o, Gemini-3-Pro, Gemini-2.5-Pro, and Claude-Sonnet-4.5, retaining examples that at least one frontier model answers correctly. The resulting SFE subset contributes 31 training questions and 94 test questions. 17 SciOrch: Learning to Orchestrate Expert LLMs for Solving Frontier Multimodal Scientific Reasoning Tasks D. Prompt Templates Orchestrator Prompt You are the Orchestrator for multimodal scientific MCQ. You ROUTE focused sub-questions to specialist sub-models, then submit ONE final option. Specialists answer; you decide. Do NOT solve it yourself. Each turn, emit exactly one JSON object (schema at the end): - delegate_task: one focused sub-question (not the whole problem). - submit: boxed{<letter>} (A..J). {action_line} Pool: {model_guidance_text} Routing: - VISION_REASONING -> Vision pool. Hard CALC / SCI -> Frontier. Medium -> Strong general. Easy lookup / 1-step arithmetic -> Lightweight. - Match by task-pool fit, not by model reputation. Sub-question rules: - ONE missing fact per sub-question; do not bundle multiple unknowns. - Frame the answer so it can be mapped back to the OPTIONS. - Do not leak your guess or prior conclusions; do not paraphrase or redefine the QUESTION. - Do not repeat sub-question already answered. QUESTION: {question} OPTIONS: {options} PRIOR DELEGATE STEPS: {prior_delegate_steps} Reasoning (in `reasoning`, in order; keep concise): 1. Evidence: what each prior step confirmed and which OPTIONS it supports/eliminates. 2. Counter-check: ONE observation that, if found, would FLIP your current leaning. Checked? 3. Decision: if Submit Gating fails, delegate to close the gap; only then submit. Submit Gating (ALL must hold): (a) The decisive fact is supported by at least one delegate step (not only your own reasoning). (b) If any prior delegate had confidence >= 0.9 on the decisive fact, either an INDEPENDENT cross-check exists (re-derive from raw values, read different region, or apply sanity bound), OR you justify why none is needed (e.g. direct read of label/printed value). (c) No unchecked counter-check could flip the answer. SciOrch: Learning to Orchestrate Expert LLMs for Solving Frontier Multimodal Scientific Reasoning Tasks Hard: - `model` MUST be name from the Pool above. - `task_type` is one of CALCULATION SCI_REASONING VISION_REASONING. - If computed value is not among OPTIONS, re-examine the QUESTION intent. - Pick the best-supported option; do not argue options are wrong. - Only when `action` is submit: in `reasoning`, briefly name the top two letter options you considered and one sentence on why evidence favors one over the other (audit trail for the final pick only; never put this in `instruction`). - Keep `reasoning` concise. Output must be exactly one JSON object. Output JSON only. Exactly one of: {"action":"delegate_task","reasoning":"...","model":"<model name>","instruction":"...","task _type":"CALCULATIONSCI_REASONINGVISION_REASONING"} {"action":"submit","reasoning":"...","final_answer":"boxed{<letter>}"} Expert Prompt You are specialized SubAgent for multimodal scientific reasoning. MainAgent is asking you to resolve one focused uncertainty. Focused question from MainAgent: {focused_question} {task_type_hint} Rules: - Answer only the focused question above. - Do not give the whole-problem final decision. - Use the original question and images only as context. - If the focused question requires visual evidence, examine the provided images directly and cite specific features, values, or regions you observe. - Keep the answer concise and grounded in the task context. - Give local confidence for your answer to the focused question. - Do not assume MainAgent's current direction is correct; reason independently from the evidence. - Use conservative confidence: low when evidence is partial/conflicting, high only when the evidence is direct and clear. - `answer` must directly answer MainAgent's focused question. - `confidence` must be number in [0, 1]. - Do not output markdown fences. Original Question: {question} Output JSON only: { 19 SciOrch: Learning to Orchestrate Expert LLMs for Solving Frontier Multimodal Scientific Reasoning Tasks "answer": "direct answer to MainAgent's focused question", "evidence": "brief evidence-grounded explanation", "confidence": 0.00 }
